bibliographic_data.title: Evaluating the Feasibility of Augmented Reality to Support Communication Access for Deaf Students in Experiential Higher Education Contexts
bibliographic_data.abstract: Deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) students often experience communication barriers in higher education, particularly in experiential learning environments such as laboratories. Traditional accessibility services require DHH students to divide their attention between critical tasks, potential safety hazards, instructional materials, and access providers, creating trade-offs between safety and equitable communication. This study investigates Augmented Reality Real-Time Access for Education (ARRAE), an ecosystem based on augmented reality (AR) smart glasses, as a potential intervention for laboratory-based environments. By overlaying interpreters or captions directly into a student’s field of view, AR enables the integration of accessibility into hands-on learning without compromising safety or comprehension.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study evaluates Augmented Reality Real-Time Access for Education (ARRAE) as a potential intervention to support communication access for deaf students in laboratory-based environments.
core_content.key_methodology: An empirical study with 12 DHH participants was conducted to evaluate how AR-mediated access influences visual attention patterns and perceived cognitive load during hands-on tasks.
outcomes: AR-mediated communication shows strong potential to improve attention management and communication accessibility in experiential learning environments.
Participants identified several design and ergonomic challenges, including display positioning, visual fatigue, and compatibility with hearing devices.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper evaluates the feasibility of using Augmented Reality (AR) to support communication access for deaf students in laboratory-based higher education settings. Traditional accessibility services often require DHH students to divide their attention between critical tasks, safety hazards, instructional materials, and access providers, creating trade-offs between safety and equitable communication. The study investigates AR as a potential intervention by overlaying interpreters or captions directly into the student’s field of view, enabling integration of accessibility without compromising safety or comprehension. Through an empirical study with 12 DHH participants, it was found that AR-mediated communication has strong potential to improve attention management and communication accessibility in experiential learning environments. However, participants highlighted several design challenges such as display positioning, visual fatigue, and compatibility with hearing devices.
